title: Hoya alexicaca
intro: The Hoya alexicaca, also known as the "Waxflower," is a type of climbing succulent plant belonging to the Apocynaceae family. Traditionally, Hoya alexicaca has been used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Hoya alexicaca include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal preparations, Hoya alexicaca is often made into teas, infusions, or tinctures, and is also used in traditional remedies such as cough syrups and expectorants.
taxonomy: Hoya alexicaca is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. This plant is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The genus Hoya belongs to the subfamily Asclepiadoideae, which comprises of plants with complex flowers and unique seed capsules. Hoya alexicaca is further classified as a species within the Hoya genus, specifically in the section Hoya. The plant is also sometimes referred to by its synonym, Hoya sp. 'Alexicaca', which is a common name used in horticulture.

side_effects: Hoya alexicaca contains a compound that may cause skin irritation or allergic reactions in some individuals, leading to redness, itching, and inflammation. It may also interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and diabetes medications, affecting their efficacy and increasing the risk of side effects.
